commonName: Nasturtium (not to be confused with Nasturtium officinale
or other plants casually called nasturtium — Tropaeolum majus is the common garden nasturtium

range.nativeRange: South America — Peru, Ecuador, and the surrounding Andean region; introduced to Europe in the late 16th century and now cultivated worldwide as an edible ornamental annual

usNativeStatus.description: Not native to the US; introduced and widely cultivated as a warm-season annual across all regions; does not naturalize in zones below 9 — killed by frost and does not establish persistent wild populations in temperate climates

invasive.description: No; not invasive anywhere in the lower 48 US states; annual frost kill prevents establishment in most of the country; in frost-free zones (southern California, southern Florida, Hawaii) it can persist year-round but is not listed on any state noxious weed list or USDA invasive species registry; no regulatory concern

zone.description: Tender annual in all zones below 9; grown as a warm-season annual from last frost to first frost; in Zone 6a, reliably productive from late May through October frost; in Zone 8+, can persist as a short-lived perennial but is typically grown as an annual for best performance

description.height: Highly habit-dependent: compact/bush types (e.g., 'Alaska', 'Jewel Mix', 'Whirlybird') grow 12-15" tall and mound outward; trailing/climbing types (e.g., 'Empress of India', 'Gleam') sprawl or climb 5-8 feet given support or space; select the habit deliberately for your placement
description.spread: Compact types: 12-18"; trailing types: 4-8 ft spread along the ground or over a fence; spreads through the season as the plant matures
description.spacing: Compact types: 10-12" apart; trailing types: 18-24" apart; thin ruthlessly — overcrowding reduces bloom
description.growthRate: Fast; germinates in 7-10 days in warm soil; reaches flowering size in about 5-6 weeks from germination
description.timeToMaturity: First flowers typically 5-6 weeks from germination; in Zone 6a, flowering begins in late June from a late-May sowing and continues until hard frost (around October 10)
description.lifespan: Tender annual — frost-killed; does not self-sow reliably in zones below 8 (seeds do not overwinter through hard freezes with confidence; treat as a direct-sow annual each season)
description.deciduousEvergreen: Annual — the whole plant collapses at first hard frost

lightAndWater.sun: Full sun to part shade; blooms most prolifically in full sun (6+ hours); tolerates part shade but flower production decreases; unlike many edible flowers, performs acceptably in bright part shade, making it flexible for placement near taller plants
lightAndWater.water: Low to moderate; drought tolerant once established; actually blooms better with some water stress — consistent moisture without waterlogging is ideal; in wet conditions, slugs can become an issue

drainagePreference.description: Well-drained to average; tolerates a wide range of soil drainage as long as the site is not waterlogged; poor drainage in heavy soil can promote slug damage and stem rot in cool, wet weather; good drainage is ideal but not strictly required for this adaptable annual

claySoilPerformance.description: Good — nasturtiums tolerate heavy clay soils well; the poor, lean conditions of unimproved clay actually encourage flowering over leaf production by limiting available nitrogen; clay's tendency to stay moist is acceptable as long as the site is not perpetually waterlogged; no amendment needed for clay itself — the plant thrives in it

soil.texturePreference: Poor, lean, infertile soil is actively preferred for flowering; the single most important soil rule for nasturtiums is do NOT fertilize — excess nitrogen produces enormous lush leaves and almost no flowers; the plant redirects its energy to vegetative growth when nitrogen is plentiful; heavy clay, sandy loam, and gravelly soils all work well as long as fertility is low
soil.rootCharacter: Taproot with fine fibrous lateral roots; the taproot is sensitive to disturbance — nasturtiums do not transplant well after germination; direct sow only, in their final position

bloom.bloomTime: Late June through October frost in Zone 6a (from a late-May sowing); in warmer zones, bloom begins earlier and extends longer; continuous bloom until killed by frost; no deadheading required to maintain bloom — the plant flowers freely without intervention, though removing spent flowers can modestly extend production
bloom.bloomDescription: Five-petaled flowers 1.5-2.5" wide; colors range across orange, yellow, red, mahogany, cream, and bicolors depending on cultivar; each flower has a distinctive backward-projecting nectar spur — a hollow tube behind the flower that holds a small drop of sweet nectar, which can be sucked out directly; the flowers are bilaterally symmetrical and face upward or outward; some cultivars (e.g., 'Whirlybird') hold flowers upright and facing up, making them ideal for cutting; the warm-toned palette is bold and joyful, with rich mahogany ('Empress of India') and soft peachy-cream ('Peach Melba') options at the elegant end
bloom.fragrance: Foliage faintly peppery-aromatic when bruised; flowers have a faint, pleasant sweetness; the plant as a whole is not strongly fragrant but adds subtle spice to the air when handled
bloom.pollination: Excellent insect pollinator plant — long nectar spurs attract hummingbirds where present; bees and bumblebees work the flowers; the accessible open face also attracts a wide range of smaller pollinators; self-fertile

yield.edibleParts: One of the most completely edible plants in the garden — flowers, leaves, stems, and unripe seed pods are all edible, each with a distinct use. The flavor throughout the plant is peppery and mustard-like, derived from glucosinolates (the same compounds that give mustard and watercress their heat). Flowers: strikingly beautiful in salads, used as a garnish on savory dishes, stuffed with soft cheese (fresh chevre or cream cheese) for appetizers; the petals freeze well — freeze in ice cubes for a dramatic presentation; the nectar spur can be sucked for a small, sweet drop of nectar — a fun edible moment especially for children. Leaves: used like watercress — spicy, peppery, satisfying; add to salads, sandwiches, and wraps; nasturtium pesto (leaves blended with olive oil, garlic, parmesan, and pine nuts or walnuts) is excellent — deeply peppery and complex. Stems: edible, similar flavor to the leaves. Unripe seed pods: the highest-value harvest — pick seed pods while still green and plump (before they harden and develop a fibrous outer coat); pickle in brine with vinegar, salt, dill, and capers if available — known as "poor man's capers" and an excellent substitute that outperforms commercial capers in sharpness; the flavor is bracing, mustardy, and excellent on pizza, in pasta, or in any application that calls for capers
yield.harvestSeason: Flowers and leaves: throughout the growing season from first bloom until frost; seed pods: midsummer onward — watch for the green pods forming behind spent flowers and harvest promptly before they begin to dry out
yield.medicinalUses: Historically used in folk medicine as an antiseptic and expectorant; the glucosinolate chemistry gives the plant genuine antimicrobial properties, though it is not commonly used in formal modern herbal medicine; the peppery compounds are known to have some antibacterial effect; primarily valued culinarily rather than medicinally
yield.craftOtherYield: Flowers can be pressed for paper crafts and dried for potpourri; the vivid colors fade somewhat when dried but remain usable decoratively; the intense color of fresh petals is used as a natural food colorant

cautionsAndHazards.toxicityPeople: Non-toxic; entirely edible and widely eaten; glucosinolates can cause mild digestive upset in very large quantities — normal culinary use is safe; individuals on blood-thinning medications should note that the plant contains Vitamin K
cautionsAndHazards.toxicityAnimals: Generally considered non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses; the peppery flavor discourages most animals from eating it in volume; not a significant concern
cautionsAndHazards.allelopathy: None documented; considered a neutral to beneficial garden companion

roles: Food (fully edible — flowers, leaves, stems, seed pods)

Trap Crop (one of the most effective aphid trap crops in the annual garden — aphids, particularly black bean aphid / blackfly, are powerfully attracted to nasturtiums; deploy as a sacrificial lure to draw aphids away from vulnerable crops)
Ground Cover (the dense sprawling foliage of trailing types covers ground quickly, suppressing weeds)
Beauty (bold ornamental color contribution to edible beds)
companionPlants: Beans (nasturtium draws blackfly away from bean crops — classic pairing; plant nasturtiums near but not against beans so aphids are lured away without creating a reservoir immediately adjacent); cucumbers

and zucchini (same trap-crop logic — aphid protection for cucurbits); tomatoes; radishes; any brassica crop (though nasturtiums are themselves in a related glucosinolate-producing family, they do not compete with brassicas and may confuse cabbage white butterflies); calendula (excellent companion in any insectary planting)
antagonists: Avoid planting nasturtiums where aphid pressure will cross directly into vulnerable crops — the trap-crop function works best when nasturtiums are placed at the margin of a bed or slightly upwind of the plants being protected
not interplanted; do not fertilize surrounding soil — fertilizer benefits neighboring plants but risks spurring excessive nasturtium leaf growth at the expense of flowers
permaculture.coppiceable: No; annual — remove plants after frost; no need for midsummer cutback

wildlife.attracts: Bees and bumblebees (nectar spur and open flower face), hoverflies (adult hoverflies visit for nectar; their larvae prey on aphids — a useful secondary benefit, though nasturtiums also attract aphids, which can ironically support the hoverfly population), butterflies; hummingbirds where present (especially during migration in the eastern US)
wildlife.hostPlantFor: Cabbage white butterfly (Pieris rapae) — nasturtiums are in the same glucosinolate-producing plant group and cabbage whites will oviposit on nasturtiums; this can serve as an incidental trap crop for brassica beds, though caterpillar damage to nasturtiums themselves can be significant if populations build; also a major host for black bean aphid (Aphis fabae) — see companion planting notes and cross-reference to Vicia faba (fava bean) profile for the bean blackfly connection

deer.description: Good — the peppery glucosinolate chemistry of the foliage is generally unpalatable to deer; nasturtiums are not a preferred browse and are considered lower priority in a deer-pressured garden; not deer-proof but unlikely to be targeted preferentially when other options are present; reasonable to use in any garden with moderate to high deer pressure across the eastern and Midwest US

salt.description: Moderate; tolerates incidental road salt exposure but is not a salt-specialist plant; as a warm-season annual sown after last frost, it avoids the worst of winter salt accumulation in most planting scenarios

drought.description: Good once established; short drought periods are tolerated well and may improve flowering; extended drought reduces leaf size and vigor; water during establishment (first 2-3 weeks) and during fruit set if harvesting seed pods

tolerances.other: Intolerant of frost — do not sow outdoors until after last frost in any zone; very heat tolerant once established; no cold stratification needed; does not tolerate waterlogging

maintenance.maintenanceLevel: Very low — one of the easiest annual food plants available; direct sow, thin, and largely leave alone
maintenance.pruningNotes: No pruning required for bloom; deadheading can marginally extend the flush of new blooms but is not necessary — nasturtiums bloom freely throughout the season without intervention; trim back trailing types if they are crowding neighboring plants; remove aphid-infested stems and knock off colonies with a sharp blast of water when conducting trap-crop management — monitor nasturtiums weekly during peak aphid season (June-August) and decide whether to manage in place or remove severely infested plants before aphid pressure spreads
propagationMethods: Seed — direct sow only; sow seeds 0.5-1" deep directly in their final position after last frost

when soil has warmed; seeds are large and easy to handle; soak seeds overnight in water before sowing to improve germination speed; germination occurs in 7-10 days in warm soil; thin to final spacing when seedlings are 2-3" tall; do not start indoors — the taproot resents transplanting and indoor-started seedlings almost always perform worse than direct-sown plants

cultivarNotes:
The choice of compact vs. trailing habit is the most important cultivar decision — choose before purchasing seeds. Compact types (12-15") are better for formal edging, bed interplanting, and any situation where spreading is undesirable; trailing/climbing types (5-8 ft) are spectacular when allowed to cascade over the edge of a raised bed, tumble down a bank, or scramble over a low fence. All cultivars listed below perform well across Zones 3-9 as warm-season annuals — the primary variable is length of growing season, not winter hardiness.
**Compact types:** 'Alaska' — distinctive variegated cream-and-green leaves make this ornamentally valuable even when not in bloom; mixed flower colors; excellent for front-of-bed placement in any zone; 'Jewel Mix' — classic cottage-garden mix of orange, yellow, and red on compact plants; widely available and reliable everywhere; 'Whirlybird' — unique upward-facing flowers (most nasturtiums face outward or downward) in a range of colors; the upfacing habit makes it ideal for cut flower use and for beds where the flower faces will be visible from above; 'Peach Melba' — cream flowers with a vivid red center blotch; elegant and unusual; excellent for a refined color palette.
**Trailing types:** 'Empress of India' — one of the most beautiful and historic nasturtiums; deep crimson-scarlet flowers against very dark blue-green leaves; richly ornamental; 'Gleam' — semi-double flowers in mixed warm tones; one of the most vigorous trailing types; excellent for covering a large area; 'Tip Top' — semi-trailing, mixed colors, considered more heat-tolerant than average and a good choice for Zone 8+ where summer heat can stress nasturtiums.

suggestedUse: trap-crop planting around beans and cucumbers
annual ground cover in food forest margins
cut flower garden; one of the most genuinely productive edible-flower annuals available — suitable for any gardener who wants both ornamental impact and real culinary yield
gardenUse.designPlacement: Sow compact types as edging plants in kitchen gardens and vegetable beds to provide continuous edible flower harvest and aphid trap-crop service; trailing types cascade beautifully over the edge of raised beds, retaining walls, or fences — place them where they have room to sprawl; nasturtiums perform best in full sun with poor soil, so they are ideal for hot, lean, unimproved areas of the garden that other plants struggle in; the trap-crop function is most effective when nasturtiums are placed at bed margins or slightly upwind of the crop being protected (beans, cucurbits, brassicas); avoid heavily shaded or highly fertile locations — shade reduces bloom and rich soil produces all leaves and no flowers; nasturtiums can also be direct-seeded into food forest floors or at guild margins as an annual ground layer before perennial ground covers establish
gardenUse.acquisitionStatus: To source (inexpensive annual from seed — available at any garden center or by mail order; seeds are very affordable in bulk; sow directly)

missouriBotanicalGarden.url: https://www.missouribotanicalgarden.org/PlantFinder/PlantFinderDetails.aspx?taxonid=264612 (cultivar group page — no species-level MBG page exists; this links to the cultivar group entry)

pennsylvaniaHegins.nativeToSite: No — South American origin; not native to Pennsylvania or any part of eastern North America
pennsylvaniaHegins.paInvasiveStatus: Not regulated in Pennsylvania; not on the PA Dept. of Agriculture noxious weed list or DCNR invasive species list; frost-killed annually and does not self-sow reliably through Zone 6a winters
pennsylvaniaHegins.drainage: Hegins red clay is nearly ideal for nasturtiums — the poor, lean, slightly acidic clay suppresses vegetative lushness and encourages flowering; do not amend the soil before sowing and do not fertilize; in-ground planting is perfectly suitable; raised beds are unnecessary for this species
pennsylvaniaHegins.soil: Hegins red clay is effectively ideal — low fertility, slightly acidic, no amendment required; do not add compost, manure, or fertilizer to nasturtium planting areas at this site
pennsylvaniaHegins.cultivarNotes: 'Alaska' (compact, ornamental leaves) and 'Empress of India' (trailing, dramatic color) are the two highest-value selections for the Hegins garden. Start with both to cover both habits. The full growing season (late May to mid-October) gives ample time for either type to reach full maturity and produce heavily.
pennsylvaniaHegins.designPlacement: Most useful in the vegetable and herb beds — sow compact types as edging in the kitchen garden zone (Section B) for continuous edible flower harvest and aphid trap-crop service; trailing types can cascade over the edge of any raised beds in Section C (south-facing, warm — ideal microclimate) where heat and sun will maximize bloom; avoid Section A (north-facing, partial shade, beauty-first — the bold informal character of nasturtiums does not suit the formal palette there, and the partial shade will reduce bloom); the trap-crop function is particularly valuable near Vicia faba (fava beans) — interplant at guild margins where bean crops are grown
pennsylvaniaHegins.siteObservations: Hegins red clay is nearly ideal for nasturtiums — poor, lean, slightly acidic clay will suppress vegetative lushness and encourage flowering; do not amend the soil before sowing and do not fertilize; the Zone 6a season (late May through October frost) gives approximately 4.5 months of productivity; deer pressure is manageable given the peppery foliage chemistry; aphid trap-crop deployment should be planned consciously — monitor weekly and act before aphid populations build to damaging levels; hummingbirds rare at Hegins but possible on migration
